title: Personnel Qualification and Radiation Control Regulations
narrationScript: Industrial radiography is one of the few trades where federal regulation decides who may do the work at all. This lesson walks the qualification ladder of 10 CFR 34.43 and then maps the regulations you will apply for the rest of your career. Layer one is certification: a written examination through a certifying entity — ASNT's IRRSP program or an Agreement State program — backed by documented formal training and on-the-job experience, with the card good for three to five years. Layer two belongs to your employer: instruction in Parts 19, 20, and 34, the DOT rules, and the licensee's operating and emergency procedures, a written or oral exam, then a hands-on practical on the actual equipment. Assistants skip the card but need a written test, a practical, and personal supervision — a radiographer on site, available, and watching. Layer three never ends: refresher training every twelve months, a performance audit every six, records kept three years. Know which regulation answers which question, because inspectors — and enforcement — assume you do.

sections.heading: Qualification Is a Regulatory Requirement, Not a Company Policy
sections.body:
A radiography source can deliver a lethal dose in minutes, and the case histories behind this course — burned hands, lost fingers, fatal overexposures — trace again and again to people who were not trained, not examined, or not supervised. That is why qualification in this trade is written into law. Since 1999, federal rules have prohibited anyone from acting as an industrial radiographer until they hold a **certification** issued through a recognized certifying entity *and* complete their employer's own training and examinations. There are no grandfather clauses and no waivers for experience alone.

Think of qualification as three layers, each required, none substituting for another:

- **Layer one — certification**: a national written examination proving you know radiation safety.
- **Layer two — licensee qualification**: your employer trains and examines you on its license, its procedures, and its equipment.
- **Layer three — maintenance**: refresher training, performance audits, and records that keep the qualification alive.

The federal rules live in 10 CFR 34.43; in Agreement States, compatible state regulations impose the same requirements in substance. This lesson walks the three layers, then maps the wider body of radiation-control regulations you will apply on every job.

sections.heading: Layer One — Certification Through a Certifying Entity
sections.body:
Before you may act as a radiographer, 10 CFR 34.43(a) requires certification through a **certifying entity** meeting the criteria of Appendix A to Part 34. Two kinds of entity qualify: an independent certifying organization — in practice **ASNT's Industrial Radiography Radiation Safety Personnel (IRRSP) program**, with examinations for radioactive-material (gamma) radiography and for X-ray radiography — or an **Agreement State** that operates its own recognized certification program.

To sit for the examination you must document **formal radiation safety training** and a **period of on-the-job experience**; the certifying entity verifies both before you test. The written examination covers the topic list in Appendix A — fundamentals of radiation safety, radiation detection instruments, radiographic equipment, the pertinent federal regulations, and **case histories of radiography accidents**. That list is the outline this course follows: master this material honestly and the certification exam holds no surprises.

Certification runs for a fixed term of **3 to 5 years** — IRRSP cards are issued for 5 — and must be renewed. The card is yours, not your employer's: it travels with you from company to company and is recognized across jurisdictions. What it cannot do is put you to work by itself. That is where layer two begins.

sections.caption: The qualification pathway of 10 CFR 34.43: certification through a certifying entity, licensee-specific training and examinations, then a maintenance loop of refresher training, performance audits, and records.

sections.heading: Layer Two — Licensee Training, Examination, and the Practical
sections.body:
**The card is necessary, never sufficient.** Certification proves general radiation safety knowledge; it says nothing about your employer's specific license conditions, devices, or procedures. So 10 CFR 34.43(a) continues: before you act as a radiographer, the licensee must give you **copies of and instruction in** the documents that will govern your work —

- 10 CFR Part 34 (or the state equivalent),
- the worker-instruction requirements of 10 CFR 19.12 and the applicable sections of 10 CFR Part 20,
- the applicable DOT transport rules, and
- the licensee's own **operating and emergency procedures**.

You then demonstrate understanding of the license and procedures by a **written or oral examination**. Next comes hardware: training in the licensee's exposure devices and sealed sources, the **daily inspection** of devices and associated equipment, and the use of radiation survey instruments — proven by a **hands-on practical examination**.

In practice the pipeline looks like this: a formal radiation safety course (the long-standing industry benchmark is **40 hours** of classroom training), a documented stretch of supervised on-the-job training — commonly about two months — and then the examinations. Exact hour requirements are set by your regulatory jurisdiction, so your license and state regulations are the final word. And because layer two is licensee-specific, **changing employers means requalifying** on the new company's procedures and equipment, card or no card.

sections.heading: Radiographer's Assistants and Personal Supervision
sections.body:
The regulations deliberately leave a working entry path into the trade: the **radiographer's assistant**. An assistant needs no certification card. Instead, 10 CFR 34.43(b) requires the assistant to receive copies of and instruction in the same governing documents, develop competence with the exposure devices, source-handling tools, and survey instruments they will actually use, and then pass two tests: a **written test** — an oral examination does not qualify an assistant — and a **practical examination** on that hardware.

What makes an uncertified assistant safe is the wrapper around them. Under 10 CFR 34.46, whenever an assistant uses an exposure device, handles sources or source-handling tools, or performs the survey that confirms the source has returned to its shield, the assistant must be under **personal supervision**, and personal supervision has three required elements:

- the radiographer's **physical presence** at the site where the sources are being used,
- the radiographer's **availability to give immediate assistance** if required, and
- the radiographer **watching** the assistant perform the operation.

A phone call from town fails all three. There is also a crew-size floor: at any location other than a permanent radiographic installation, 10 CFR 34.41 requires **at least two qualified individuals** present — the radiographer plus another certified radiographer or someone who meets the assistant requirements. Working alone is permitted only inside a permanent radiographic installation equipped with the required entry controls and alarms.

sections.caption: Qualification requirements compared — 10 CFR 34.43 and 34.46 (Agreement State regulations impose compatible requirements).
rows: Required before acting — ASNT IRRSP or an Agreement State program (Part 34, Appendix A)
Exam on the license and operating/emergency procedures
Written test — an oral exam does not qualify
Training plus practical examination: devices, daily inspection, survey instruments
Competence developed under supervision, plus a practical examination on the hardware used
Personal supervision only — radiographer present, available, and watching
At intervals not to exceed 12 months
Performance inspection by the RSO or designee
During an actual operation, at intervals not to exceed 6 months

sections.heading: Layer Three — Staying Qualified: Refresher, Audits, and Records
sections.body:
Qualification decays without maintenance, and the regulations schedule the maintenance.

- **Annual refresher** — the licensee must provide refresher safety training for every radiographer and every assistant at intervals **not to exceed 12 months**.
- **Performance inspections** — the RSO or a designee must observe each radiographer and assistant during an **actual radiographic operation** at intervals **not to exceed 6 months**, checking the work against the regulations, the license, and the operating and emergency procedures. A paperwork review in the office does not satisfy this; the rule requires watching real work.
- **The lapse rule** — if an individual has not participated in radiography for more than 6 months since the last inspection, their performance must be **observed and recorded the next time they participate**.

All of it is records or it never happened: 10 CFR 34.79 requires the licensee to keep — for **3 years** after each record is made — certification documents and verification of certification status, copies and dates of written and oral tests, refresher training topics, dates, instructors, and attendees, and the items checked and any non-compliances found during performance audits.

One more set of shoulders carries the program: the **Radiation Safety Officer**. Under 10 CFR 34.42, an RSO must complete the same training and testing required of a radiographer, log **2,000 hours of hands-on experience as a qualified radiographer** in industrial radiographic operations, and hold formal training in establishing and maintaining a radiation protection program — though the regulator may accept documented equivalent qualifications.

sections.heading: The Regulations for the Control of Radiation — and How to Apply Them
sections.body:
Every requirement in this course comes from a findable place, and a working radiographer should know the map cold. Where the NRC regulates, the working set is:

- **10 CFR Part 19** — what your employer must tell you: posting NRC Form 3, instruction in hazards and protective measures, your right to request an inspection and to speak privately with inspectors.
- **10 CFR Part 20** — the standards for protection against radiation: occupational and public dose limits, monitoring, surveys, posting, and ALARA.
- **10 CFR Part 34** — everything radiography-specific: personnel qualification, exposure devices, required equipment, surveys, and reporting.
- **10 CFR Part 37** — physical protection for higher-activity sealed sources, thresholds that typical radiography sources can reach.
- **10 CFR Part 71 and the DOT rules in 49 CFR** — transport, covered in the next lesson.

In an **Agreement State**, the state radiation control agency enforces its own regulations — written to be compatible with the federal rules, most modeled on the CRCPD Suggested State Regulations — and **X-ray machines are regulated by the states everywhere**, because NRC authority covers radioactive material, not electrically generated radiation. Layered on top sit two binding documents unique to your employer: the **license and its conditions**, and the **operating and emergency procedures**. When requirements differ, the most restrictive applicable one governs your work.

Apply the regulations the way inspectors do: question first, citation second. Unannounced inspections, notices of violation, civil penalties, and license suspension or revocation are how the system enforces itself — and enforcement reaches **individuals**, not just companies. A radiographer who deliberately violates requirements or falsifies records can personally face enforcement orders, up to being barred from licensed work.

sections.caption: A radiographer's regulation map: match the operational question to the rule that answers it. Agreement State agencies enforce compatible equivalents, and X-ray machines are state-regulated everywhere.

interactive.title: Radiographer, Assistant, or Both?
interactive.intro: Every requirement below comes from 10 CFR 34.41 through 34.46. Sort each one into the role it applies to — and notice how the rules let an uncertified assistant work safely by wrapping supervision around them.

items.label: Certified through a certifying entity — IRRSP card or Agreement State program — before acting in the role
items.why: 10 CFR 34.43(a) requires certification only for radiographers. Assistants qualify through licensee instruction, a written test, and a practical exam — no card.

items.label: May prove understanding of the license and operating procedures by either a written or an oral examination
items.why: The radiographer's procedures exam may be written or oral under 34.43(a). The assistant's may not — the rule for assistants specifies a written test.

items.label: Must pass a written test on the governing documents — an oral examination is not an option
items.why: 10 CFR 34.43(b) requires assistants to demonstrate understanding by successfully completing a written test, with no oral alternative.

items.label: Handles the exposure device only with a radiographer physically at the site, available to assist immediately, and watching the work
items.why: That is personal supervision under 10 CFR 34.46 — it applies to assistants using devices, handling sources or source-handling tools, or making the source-return survey.

items.label: Annual refresher safety training at intervals not to exceed 12 months
items.why: 10 CFR 34.43 requires refresher training for each radiographer and each radiographer's assistant on the same 12-month clock.

items.label: Job performance observed by the RSO or designee during an actual radiographic operation at least every 6 months
items.why: The performance-inspection program covers both roles, and the observation must happen during real radiographic work — not a classroom review.

items.label: Completes a hands-on practical examination before being allowed to work
items.why: Both roles must pass a practical: radiographers on the licensee's devices, daily inspection, and survey instruments; assistants on the hardware they will use under supervision.

items.label: Counts toward the minimum of two qualified individuals required at a temporary job site
items.why: 10 CFR 34.41 requires the radiographer to be accompanied by at least one other certified radiographer or an individual meeting the assistant requirements — either role fills the second slot.

checkQuestions.question: Before an individual may act as a radiographer under 10 CFR 34.43, which combination is required?
options: Certification by a certifying entity alone — the IRRSP card authorizes radiography anywhere in the United States
Five years of experience as a radiographer's assistant and a letter from the RSO
Certification through a certifying entity, plus the licensee's training and written-or-oral exam on its license and procedures, plus a hands-on practical examination on its equipment
A college degree in radiologic technology and an examination administered by the NRC
checkQuestions.explanation: Qualification is layered: the Appendix A certification proves general radiation safety knowledge, and the licensee must still instruct and examine the individual on its own license, operating and emergency procedures, and equipment — finishing with a practical exam. The card alone authorizes nothing.

checkQuestions.question: A radiographer spent the last 7 months on non-radiography work, so her last documented performance inspection was 8 months ago. Under 10 CFR 34.43(d), what is required?
options: Nothing — performance inspections are only required every 12 months
Her performance must be observed and recorded the next time she participates in a radiographic operation
She must retake the IRRSP certification examination before returning to radiography
She is disqualified and must requalify from the beginning as a radiographer's assistant
checkQuestions.explanation: Performance inspections occur during actual operations at intervals not exceeding 6 months. When someone has not participated for more than 6 months since the last inspection, the rule requires observing and recording their performance on the next job — it does not invalidate their certification.

checkQuestions.question: A radiographer's assistant is cranking the source out for an exposure. Which arrangement satisfies the personal-supervision requirement of 10 CFR 34.46?
options: The radiographer is reachable by phone from the shop 20 miles away
The radiographer reviewed the job scope with the assistant that morning
A second, more experienced assistant watches the operation
The radiographer is at the site, available to give immediate assistance, and watching the assistant perform the operation
checkQuestions.explanation: Personal supervision has three required elements: the radiographer's physical presence at the site, availability to give immediate assistance, and direct observation of the assistant's work. Phone availability, a morning briefing, or another assistant satisfies none of them.

checkQuestions.question: An industrial radiography company uses Ir-192 exposure devices in an Agreement State. Day-to-day radiographic operations with that source are regulated by:
options: The state's radiation control agency, under regulations compatible with NRC requirements
The NRC directly, because sealed sources remain federal property
OSHA, because radiography is a workplace activity
No agency — sealed sources below 100 curies are exempt from licensing
checkQuestions.explanation: In an Agreement State, the state has assumed regulatory authority over byproduct material and enforces its own compatible regulations. The NRC regulates directly only in non-Agreement States; OSHA addresses other workplace hazards, and sealed radiography sources are never exempt from licensing.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.42, the qualifications of a Radiation Safety Officer for an industrial radiography licensee include:
options: Only a current IRRSP certification card
A health-physics degree and ten years of NRC employment

Completion of radiographer training and testing per 34.43(a), 2,000 hours of hands-on experience as a qualified radiographer, and formal training in establishing and maintaining a radiation protection program
Election by the radiography crew and approval by OSHA
checkQuestions.explanation: The RSO must know the job from the inside: the same training and testing as a radiographer, 2,000 hours of hands-on radiography experience, and formal radiation-protection-program training — though the regulator may consider documented equivalent alternatives.

references: 10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements (§§34.41–34.46 personnel, supervision, and training; §34.79 records; Appendix A radiographer certification)
10 CFR Part 20 — Standards for Protection Against Radiation
10 CFR Part 19 — Notices, Instructions and Reports to Workers: Inspection and Investigations
ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024) — Radiation Safety topical outline §§12.5–12.6 (personnel qualification; regulations for the control of radiation)
